text.title: Pair Programming Styles
text.summary: Four named pairing styles with roles, flow, and best-fit scenarios.

rule.statement: Driver writes code; navigator reviews in real time, thinks strategically, and spots errors. Swap roles every 15–30 minutes.
rule.rationale: Tactical-strategic split keeps one person focused on syntax and one on design, reducing both bugs and architectural drift.

list.item: Driver: types, verbalizes reasoning, does not make independent design decisions without navigator input.
Navigator: does not dictate every keystroke; guides direction, notes next steps, thinks about tests and edge cases.

rule.statement: Person A writes a failing test; Person B makes it pass with the simplest code; Person B writes the next failing test; repeat.
rule.rationale: Forces TDD discipline, keeps both participants active, and ensures every production change is driven by a failing test.

rule.statement: Commit after every green: message prefix "test:" for the test turn, "feat:" for the make-pass turn.
rule.rationale: Micro-commits make it trivial to revert a bad make-pass attempt without losing the test.

section.title: Strong-Style

rule.statement: For any idea to go from a person's head to the computer, it must go through someone else's hands — the navigator cannot type.
rule.rationale: Forces explicit verbalization of intent, making implicit assumptions visible and teachable.

section.p: Best for: mentoring, onboarding, forcing an agent to explain before acting.

section.title: Tour Guide

rule.statement: Expert navigates a newcomer through an unfamiliar codebase; newcomer drives. Expert explains architecture, history, and gotchas without typing code.
rule.rationale: Newcomer learns faster by doing than by watching; expert knowledge is transferred verbally rather than silently.

section.p: Best for: codebase onboarding, knowledge documentation, agent-as-tour-guide (Read/Glob/Grep only).

rule.statement: Limit pairing sessions to 2 hours for remote, 4–6 hours total per day with breaks every 25 minutes.
rule.rationale: Cognitive load from sustained shared attention causes quality to degrade; breaks restore focus.

antipattern.description: Keyboard hog: one person always drives.
antipattern.reason: Navigator disengage and learning stops; use a timer-based rotation.

antipattern.description: Backseat driver: navigator dictates every keystroke.
antipattern.reason: Driver loses ownership and stops thinking; navigator should guide direction, not transcription.
Without think-aloud, the navigator cannot review intent; pair becomes two people on one computer.
